content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 29 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 115v–115r | **Source Images:** DEU_029_page_001.jpg, DEU_029_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 161–162 | **Images:** DEU_029_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_029_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Moses summoned all Israel and said to them: "You have seen all that the LORD did before your eyes in the land of Egypt, to Pharaoh and to all his servants and to all his land,

**2** the great trials that your eyes saw, the signs, and those great wonders.

**3** But to this day the LORD has not given you a heart to understand or eyes to see or ears to hear.

**4** I have led you forty years in the wilderness. Your clothes have not worn out on you, and your sandals have not worn off your feet.

**5** You have not eaten bread, and you have not drunk wine or strong drink, so that you might know that I am the LORD your God.

**6** And when you came to this place, Sihon the king of Heshbon and Og the king of Bashan came out against us for battle, and we struck them down.

**7** We took their land and gave it for an inheritance to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of the Manassites.

**8** Therefore keep the words of this covenant and do them, that you may prosper in all that you do.

**9** "You are standing today, all of you, before the LORD your God: the heads of your tribes, your elders, and your officers, all the men of Israel,

**10** your little ones, your wives, and the sojourner who is in your camp, from the one who chops your wood to the one who draws your water,

**11** so that you may enter into the sworn covenant of the LORD your God, which the LORD your God is making with you today,

**12** that he may establish you today as his people, and that he may be your God, as he promised you, and as he swore to your fathers, to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob.

**13** It is not with you alone that I am making this sworn covenant,

**14** but with whoever is standing here with us today before the LORD our God, and with whoever is not here with us today.

**15** "You know how we lived in the land of Egypt, and how we came through the midst of the nations through which you passed.

**16** And you have seen their detestable things, their idols of wood and stone, of silver and gold, which were among them.

**17** Beware lest there be among you a man or woman or clan or tribe whose heart is turning away today from the LORD our God to go and serve the gods of those nations. Beware lest there be among you a root bearing poisonous and bitter fruit,

**18** one who, when he hears the words of this sworn covenant, blesses himself in his heart, saying, 'I shall be safe, though I walk in the stubbornness of my heart.' This would lead to the sweeping away of moist and dry alike.

**19** The LORD will not be willing to forgive him, but rather the anger of the LORD and his jealousy will smoke against that man, and every curse written in this book will settle on him, and the LORD will blot out his name from under heaven.

**20** And the LORD will single him out from all the tribes of Israel for calamity, in accordance with all the curses of the covenant written in this Book of the Instruction.

**21** "And the generation to come, your children who rise up after you, and the foreigner who comes from a far land, will see the afflictions of that land and the sicknesses with which the LORD has made it sick,

**22** the whole land burned out with brimstone and salt, nothing sown and nothing growing, where no plant sprouts, an overthrow like that of Sodom and Gomorrah, Admah and Zeboiim, which the LORD overthrew in his anger and wrath —

**23** all the nations will ask, 'Why has the LORD done thus to this land? What caused the heat of this great anger?'

**24** Then people will say, 'It is because they abandoned the covenant of the LORD, the God of their fathers, which he made with them when he brought them out of the land of Egypt,

**25** and went and served other gods and worshiped them, gods whom they had not known and whom he had not allotted to them.

**26** Therefore the anger of the LORD was kindled against this land, bringing upon it all the curses written in this book,

**27** and the LORD uprooted them from their land in anger and fury and great wrath, and cast them into another land, as they are today.'

**28** "The hidden things belong to the LORD our God, but the revealed things belong to us and to our children forever, that we may do all the words of this instruction."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**LOGOS — Deuteronomy 29**

**Chapter numbering:** In the Masoretic tradition and Leningrad Codex, the verse that begins "These are the words of the covenant" is the last verse of chapter 28 (28:69 in Hebrew). English Bibles typically begin chapter 29 one verse earlier. The LOGOS translation follows the Masoretic/Hebrew chapter division, so what English Bibles number 29:1 is here the first verse of chapter 29, and the chapter has 28 verses (English versifications have 29). The LXX Vaticanus paragraphing is broadly consistent with the Masoretic structure here.

**"A heart to understand" (v. 3):** *lev da'at ve-einayim lirot ve-oznayyim lishmoa* — "a heart to know, and eyes to see, and ears to hear." The threefold formula (heart/eyes/ears) frames understanding as a comprehensive faculty — cognitive, perceptual, and auditory. The LXX has *kardian eidenai kai ophthalmous blepein kai ota akouein* (καρδίαν εἰδέναι καὶ ὀφθαλμοὺς βλέπειν καὶ ὦτα ἀκούειν), exactly matching the Hebrew triad.

**"Clothes have not worn out" (v. 4):** The preservation of clothing and sandals in the wilderness functions as a covenant sign — miraculous sustenance without the normal provisions of agricultural civilization. The LXX renders *to himation sou ou katepalaiothe apo sou* (τὸ ἱμάτιόν σου οὐ κατεπαλαιώθη ἀπό σου), "your garment did not grow old from you," closely equivalent.

**"Root bearing poisonous and bitter fruit" (v. 17):** *shoresh poreh rosh ve-la'anah* — "a root bearing *rosh* and wormwood." *Rosh* (רֹאשׁ) is a poisonous plant (possibly hemlock or another toxic herb); *la'anah* (wormwood) is notoriously bitter. Together they form a merism for deadly, unpalatable poison. I render "poisonous and bitter fruit" to preserve both qualities. The LXX has *riza en chole kai pikria* (ῥίζα ἐν χολῇ καὶ πικρία, "a root in gall and bitterness"), choosing *chole* (gall/bile) rather than a specific plant name.

**"Moist and dry alike" (v. 18):** *le-safer ha-ravah et ha-tsemah* — literally "to sweep away the watered with the thirsty." This cryptic phrase (one of the most debated in Deuteronomy) appears to mean that the self-deceiver's rebellion would bring destruction on the innocent along with the guilty — the "moist" (perhaps the righteous) being swept away with the "dry" (the wicked). Alternatively, it refers to total destruction (leaving nothing alive, whether moist or dry). The LXX renders *ina me synapolesetai ho hamartolon ton anamartetous* (ἵνα μὴ συναπολέσῃ ὁ ἁμαρτωλὸν τὸν ἀναμαρτήτους, "lest the sinner destroy the innocent"), which is an interpretive expansion rather than a close rendering.

**"Whoever is not here with us today" (v. 14):** *ve-et asher eynenu po* — a striking theological formulation: the covenant extends to future generations not physically present at the ratification ceremony. The LXX renders *kai tous me onta hode met hemon semeron* (καὶ τοὺς μὴ ὄντα ὧδε μεθ᾽ ἡμῶν σήμερον), "and those not being here with us today," closely equivalent.

**Sodom and Gomorrah, Admah and Zeboiim (v. 22):** All four cities of the plain are named, recalling Genesis 14 and 19. Admah and Zeboiim appear together also in Hosea 11:8, where God expresses reluctance to destroy Israel as he did those cities. The LXX preserves all four names: *Sodoma kai Gomorra, Admam kai Seboim* (Σόδομα καὶ Γόμορρα, Αδαμὰ καὶ Σεβωίμ), matching the Hebrew.

**Ha-nistarot la-YHWH / The hidden things (v. 28):** The chapter's climactic final verse is one of the most theologically weighted statements in Deuteronomy: *ha-nistarot la-YHWH Eloheinu ve-ha-niglot lanu u-le-vaneinu ad olam la'asot et-kol divrei ha-torah ha-zot* — "The hidden things belong to the LORD our God, but the revealed things belong to us and to our children forever, to do all the words of this instruction." The verse acknowledges divine hiddenness and mystery while insisting that what has been revealed is sufficient and binding. The LXX renders *ta krypta* (τὰ κρυπτά, "the hidden things") and *ta phanera* (τὰ φανερά, "the revealed things"), faithfully capturing the contrast. Unusually, in many Hebrew manuscripts the phrase *lanu u-le-vaneinu* ("to us and to our children") has extraordinary points (*nequdot*) placed above the letters by the Masoretes, marking it as a tradition of uncertain reading — a scribal note of mystery within the very verse that speaks of what is hidden.

— LOGOS
